title: Sudan Archives: How to Build a World With Violin and Chaos
author: Allastair Voss
body:
## Sudan Archives: How to Build a World With Violin and Chaos

[img:/assets/images/artists/sudan-archives-natural-brown-prom-queen.jpg]

There is a moment in "NBPQ (Topless)" where the violin cuts through everything, production, tempo, voice, and you realize you are listening to something assembled differently from anything else in the R&B orbit. That is what Sudan Archives does. Cincinnati-born, LA-based Brittney Parks writes music that refuses the usual containers. The refusal is not a position or a marketing strategy. It is a method, the way the music is actually built from first principles rather than inherited conventions.

Natural Brown Prom Queen, released on Stones Throw Records in 2022, is her most fully realized statement. Across 18 tracks she weaves self-taught violin into trap percussion, traditional West African rhythmic patterns, and electronic textures in a way that sounds both technically complex and physically immediate. "Selfish Soul" hits like a pop song and sounds like an experiment. "Freakalizer" bounces with a kind of joy that most producers would not think to pair with strings. "Home Maker" sits in a slower, more tender register, the violin carrying something the words do not need to say.

The album is named for an experience: the moment when a Black woman walks into a room as she is, without apology. Parks has said she wanted the record to feel like a coronation, not a plea. It does.

## The Violin as Architecture

What is easy to miss on first listen is how technically sophisticated the arrangements are. The violin is not decoration. It is the architecture. Parks builds from it outward, adding layers of bass, synth, and drum machine on top. She sounds less like someone who plays violin in an R&B context and more like someone who rewired R&B through violin to begin with. The distinction is fundamental. One is addition. The other is reconstruction.

The self-taught aspect of her violin technique is directly relevant to the sound. Classical training produces specific kinds of precision and specific kinds of limitation. A self-taught player approaches the instrument through feel rather than rule, discovers techniques through accident and preference rather than pedagogy, and develops a relationship with the instrument that is personal rather than institutional. Parks's violin playing has a quality of directness that trained playing often lacks. The phrasing is idiosyncratic. The intonation is sometimes intentionally roughened. These are choices, not limitations.

There is also something about Parks's voice and how it functions alongside the violin. Her singing is not separate from the instrumental work. It operates in the same register of personal directness, the same refusal of the smooth. The vocal performance and the violin performance share a quality of someone who has decided to be exactly as they are rather than as training might have made them.

The use of extended techniques, the way Parks manipulates the instrument to produce textures that orthodox violin technique would not produce, connects her playing to the experimental music tradition without placing the music inside that tradition. She is using tools from across disciplines and the result belongs to no single one of them. This refusal of category is not a marketing decision. It is an accurate description of the music.

## West Africa and the Rhythmic Foundation

The West African rhythmic influence is present throughout Natural Brown Prom Queen at a structural rather than decorative level. Parks has spoken about her research into West African musical traditions, and the polyrhythmic complexity of the album reflects a genuine engagement with those traditions rather than a surface borrowing.

This matters because a lot of music that describes itself as influenced by African musical traditions is borrowing surface textures, specific rhythmic patterns or instrumental timbres, without engaging with the underlying logic. Parks engages with the logic. The way multiple rhythmic layers interact in her arrangements, the way the violin melody sits against the drum machine, the way harmonic and rhythmic information are distributed across different instruments simultaneously, reflects an understanding of how West African music actually works rather than what it sounds like from the outside.

The trap percussion that appears throughout the album creates a productive friction with the violin and the West African rhythmic material. Trap as a production approach has its own conventions around how percussion functions, where the kicks land, how hi-hats are layered, what the ratio of space to density should be. Parks works against these conventions using the violin and the polyrhythmic material, creating an internal tension in the production that is part of what makes the album feel alive.

Stones Throw Records is the right home for this work. The label has a history of releasing music that defies easy categorization, from Madlib to J Dilla to more recent artists, and Natural Brown Prom Queen fits within that tradition of genre-refusal. The album would have been harder to place at a label committed to any single genre.

## The Coronation

Parks has described the conceptual frame of the album as a coronation rather than a plea. This distinction is worth sitting with. A plea asks permission. A coronation declares what is already true. The prom queen of the title is not aspiring to a status she does not yet hold. She is arriving at a recognition of what she is.

The emotional register of the album reflects this frame. The joy on tracks like "Freakalizer" is not the joy of someone who has been let in. It is the joy of someone who was always here and is now making that visible. The tenderness on "Home Maker" is not vulnerability in the sense of exposure to harm. It is the tenderness of someone secure enough to be gentle. The confidence runs all the way through the record. It is the organizing principle.

Parks is one of the most technically innovative artists working in the broad space of Black American music right now. The technical innovations are not ends in themselves. They are in service of an emotional and conceptual project that is clear and specific. That is the combination that makes work last.

Three years on, Natural Brown Prom Queen still sounds ahead of where the surrounding culture is. That is the mark of something real.
